front: In Java, when is a constructor executed?
back: A constructor is executed automatically when a new object is created from a class using the `new` keyword. It initializes the new object's state.

front: What is the purpose of the `super()` call in a constructor and when does it execute?
back: The `super()` call is used to invoke the constructor of the parent class. It must be the first statement in a constructor (if used) and executes before the body of the child class's constructor.

front: Describe the order of constructor execution in a class hierarchy (inheritance).
back: In inheritance, constructors execute in order from the top-most parent class down to the child class. The Java compiler automatically inserts a `super()` call if none is written, ensuring the parent constructor runs first.

front: When is the default (no-argument) constructor provided by Java executed?
back: The default constructor is executed when an object is instantiated without any arguments, e.g., `new ClassName()`. It is only provided by the compiler if the programmer has not defined any other constructors.

front: How does constructor chaining work within the same class using `this()`?
back: The `this()` call is used to invoke another constructor within the same class. It must be the first statement in a constructor. The chained constructor executes fully before the remaining code in the initial constructor runs.
